aliases: Whole Wheat Flatbread with Vegetable Curry

english: Whole Wheat Flatbread with Vegetable Curry

region_of_origin: Indian subcontinent; commonly consumed in Singapore

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a chapati entry; vegetable curry component is not separately coded but is captured in food_name and classification.
ontology.foodex2_description: Chapati — unleavened whole wheat flatbread, served with vegetable curry
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00KG covers flatbreads including chapati; vegetable curry accompaniment is not a separate FoodEx2 node; 'closest_parent' match type used as it's a general category for flatbreads.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT concept found for chapati with vegetable curry as a combined dish; null applied honestly.

main_ingredients: mixed vegetables (potato, carrot, peas, onion, tomato)

serving.description: 2 pieces of chapati with a side of vegetable curry
serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving consists of 2 pieces of chapati (approximately 60–70 g each) accompanied by a portion of vegetable curry, totalling approximately 350 g.

nutrient_source.title: Chapati with Vegetables — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: HPB Foodo lists 'Chapati W Veg' (G00010339) as a canonical entry with full macro and micronutrient data. Core macros, dietary fibre, sodium, potassium, calcium, magnesium, iron, and cholesterol are sourced directly from HPB at confidence 0.95. Saturated fat, trans fat, and vitamin C were supplemented from a secondary source at confidence 0.70; these values are consistent with ICMR-NIN reference data for similar preparations and are retained with a confidence note.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Chapati (whole wheat flatbread) — University of Sydney GI Database; Atkinson FS et al., 'International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values 2021', American Journal of Clinical Nutrition, 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: The University of Sydney GI Database reports a GI of approximately 52 (low) for whole wheat chapati. This value is used as the primary GI anchor for chapati with vegetable curry. The vegetable curry component (non-starchy vegetables, legumes, spices) does not materially raise the composite GI; the combined dish GI is estimated at 52 (low). Glycemic load per 100g calculated as: GI (52) × carbohydrate per 100g (43.1 g) / 100 = 22.4.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 430 mg/100 g exceeds the 400 mg/100 g threshold for hypertension-sensitive foods
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to one chapati and request reduced-salt preparation; the potassium content (250 mg/100 g) provides partial mitigation but does not offset the elevated sodium at this concentration.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Carbohydrate 43.1 g/100 g with GI ~52 (low); dietary fibre 6.0 g/100 g moderates but does not eliminate post-meal glucose elevation
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit intake to 1–2 chapatis per meal, pair with high-protein legume curry to blunt glycaemic response, and substitute high-GI vegetables (potato) with lower-GI alternatives such as cauliflower, green beans, or spinach.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Sodium 430 mg/100 g is elevated; saturated fat is low at 1.14 g/100 g and cholesterol is 0 mg/100 g, which are favourable cardiovascular markers
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should monitor sodium intake from this dish carefully; the low saturated fat and zero cholesterol profile are beneficial, and the high dietary fibre (6.0 g/100 g) supports LDL reduction, but sodium reduction in preparation is strongly advised.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Anti-inflammatory spices (turmeric, ginger, garlic, cumin) are beneficial; sodium 430 mg/100 g exceeds RA threshold; nightshade vegetables may aggravate symptoms in sensitive individuals
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis may benefit from the anti-inflammatory spice profile of the vegetable curry, but should reduce salt in preparation and consider substituting nightshade vegetables (potato, tomato, chilli) with cauliflower or sweet potato if nightshade sensitivity is present.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Chapati with Vegetable Curry is a traditional Indian unleavened flatbread made from whole wheat flour (atta), dry-roasted on a tawa (flat griddle), served alongside a spiced vegetable curry. The curry typically contains a mixture of potatoes, carrots, peas, onions, and tomatoes cooked with turmeric, cumin, coriander, ginger, garlic, and chilli in a small amount of oil. The dish is a staple of Indian cuisine and is widely consumed in Singapore across Indian-Muslim and South Indian communities, commonly served at lunch and dinner. Nutritionally, the dish provides 254 kcal per 100 g, with a moderate protein content of 12.3 g, low fat at 3.55 g (saturated fat 1.14 g), and a significant carbohydrate load of 43.1 g. The high dietary fibre content of 6.0 g/100 g from whole wheat and vegetables moderates the glycaemic response (GI ~52, low category). Sodium is elevated at 430 mg/100 g, which is a concern for hypertension and cardiovascular disease management. The dish is halal, vegetarian, and vegan-compatible. It is a good source of iron (2.56 mg/100 g), calcium (163 mg/100 g), magnesium (77 mg/100 g), and potassium (250 mg/100 g). The anti-inflammatory spice profile (turmeric, ginger, garlic) provides additional health benefits relevant to inflammatory conditions such as rheumatoid arthritis. Clinically, portion control and sodium reduction in preparation are the primary dietary management strategies for individuals with cardiometabolic conditions.

confidence_reason: Core macros and primary micronutrients sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo canonical entry G00010339 at confidence 0.95. Saturated fat, trans fat, and vitamin C were supplemented from a secondary source at confidence 0.70 but are consistent with ICMR-NIN reference ranges for similar preparations. GI is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database for whole wheat chapati (analogous match). Overall record confidence is high given the HPB canonical provenance for the majority of nutrient fields.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation data for chapati with vegetable curry is not available in the HPB Foodo database or ICMR-NIN tables as of 2026. 2. vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g are set to -1: these micronutrient values are not reported in the HPB Foodo entry G00010339 and are not available from ICMR-NIN for this specific combined dish preparation as of 2026. 3. cholesterol_mg is 0.0: confirmed zero per HPB Foodo source; this dish contains no animal-derived fat components in the standard preparation. 4. trans_fat_g is 0.03 g/100 g: trace level reported by secondary source; value retained as it is above zero and plausible for a small amount of cooking oil used in curry preparation. 5. Saturated fat (1.14 g/100 g), trans fat (0.03 g/100 g), and vitamin C (4.29 mg/100 g) were sourced from a secondary source (confidence 0.70) as these fields were not reported in the primary HPB entry; values are consistent with ICMR-NIN reference data for similar preparations. 6. GI value of 52 is an analogous estimate based on whole wheat chapati from the University of Sydney GI Database; the vegetable curry component does not materially alter the composite GI. GL per 100 g = 52 × 43.1 / 100 = 22.4. 7. The portion size of 350 g (2 chapatis with vegetable curry side) is sourced from a delivery platform and is used as a serving reference only; it is not used as a nutrient source.
